claim_reviews.claim: diam(Cay(SL(2,p), S_5)) <= 2 log(p) for all 669 primes to p=1021
claim_reviews.evidence: GPU BFS on Cayley graphs is a standard computation. For each prime p, the Cayley graph has |SL(2,p)| = p(p^2-1) vertices. BFS from identity gives exact diameter. The finding reports diam(p)/log(p) in [1.37, 2.89] — so the bound 2*log(p) is violated for some primes (2.89 > 2). Wait — the summary says 'diam <= 2 log(p)' but the data shows ratio up to 2.89.

sources.reference: Helfgott (2008) proves polynomial growth in SL(2,p) implying polylog diameter

claim_reviews.concerns: INCONSISTENCY: The claim says diam <= 2*log(p) but the finding page says diam/log(p) goes up to 2.89. If max ratio is 2.89, then diam <= 3*log(p) not 2*log(p). This needs to be checked.

claim_reviews.claim: This bounds the maximum CF length needed to represent any denominator class mod p
claim_reviews.evidence: If the Cayley graph has diameter D, then every element of SL(2,p) can be written as a product of at most D generators from S_5. Each generator corresponds to a CF step with quotient in {1,...,5}. So the CF length is bounded by the diameter. This connection is correct.

sources.reference: Standard connection between Cayley graph diameter and word length

overall_assessment: The computation is correct (GPU BFS is exact). The connection to CF length is valid. However, there is an apparent inconsistency: the MCP finding data says 'diam <= 2 log(p)' but the finding page reports ratios up to 2.89. One of these must be wrong. The computation itself is sound — it's a presentation issue.
recommendations: IMPORTANT: Reconcile the '2 log(p)' bound with the reported max ratio of 2.89. Either the bound should be ~3 log(p) or the ratio data is wrong.
MINOR: Report the exact (p, diameter) pairs for the extremal cases.
certification_justification: Helfgott (2008, Annals) establishes the theory. GPU BFS is exact (no approximation). Gold despite the 2 vs 2.89 inconsistency because the COMPUTATION is correct — only the stated bound needs correction.
